meta.title: F.1 How Browsers Execute Code
meta.description: Trace the six-stage browser rendering pipeline — from HTML parsing and CSSOM construction through layout, paint, and compositing — and understand the JavaScript event loop well enough to diagnose why Make previews jank, flash, or block the main thread.

blocks.prompt:
> When a user clicks "Preview" in Figma Make and a working app appears in 2 seconds, what happened between their click and the pixels on screen? List every step you can think of.

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module gives you a working model of the six-stage browser rendering pipeline and the JavaScript event loop. By the end, you'll be able to diagnose why a Make preview janks, flashes, or blocks the main thread — and explain the difference between client-side and server-side rendering as an infrastructure cost decision, not just a performance preference.

Every Make preview is the full browser execution pipeline running on untested AI-generated code — which means performance problems in generated output aren't bugs in the preview system, they are failures of code quality that the platform must either prevent or gracefully surface. Understanding it is not optional for a Platform PM — every performance complaint, every "why is preview slow," every decision about server-rendered versus client-rendered output traces back to this pipeline.

**The Browser Execution Pipeline**

When the browser receives HTML (from a server, a blob URL, or an injected string), it runs through six sequential stages:

**1. HTML Parsing → DOM Construction**
The browser reads HTML as a byte stream and tokenizes it — `<div class="app">` becomes an open-tag token with an attribute. These tokens are assembled into nodes, and nodes are assembled into a tree: the Document Object Model (DOM — a live, programmatic tree of every element on the page). Scripts and stylesheets block this process — a `<script>` tag without `async` or `defer` halts parsing entirely while the JS downloads and executes.

**2. CSSOM Construction**
In parallel with HTML parsing, the browser parses CSS into its own tree — the CSS Object Model (CSSOM — a parallel tree that maps every CSS rule to the nodes it styles). Every selector rule maps to computed styles for matching nodes. The CSSOM cannot be constructed incrementally the way the DOM can; CSS is "render-blocking" (it halts further rendering until the entire stylesheet is processed) because a later rule can override an earlier one, so the browser must process the entire stylesheet before it knows what anything looks like.

**3. Render Tree**
The DOM and CSSOM are combined into the Render Tree — a tree that contains only visible nodes with their computed styles. `display: none` nodes are excluded entirely. `visibility: hidden` nodes are included (they still take up space). This is the first structure that represents "what will actually appear on screen."

**4. Layout (Reflow)**
The browser traverses the Render Tree and calculates the exact position and size of every element. This is called layout, or reflow. Box model calculations happen here — margins, padding, width, height, flexbox and grid algorithms all resolve in this phase. Layout is expensive, especially for deeply nested trees or when triggered repeatedly by JavaScript.

**5. Paint**
Layout gives coordinates; paint fills in the pixels. The browser determines what to draw in each layer — background colors, borders, text, shadows. Paint produces a series of draw calls, not pixels directly. Complex visual effects (box-shadow, filter, opacity) can make this phase expensive.

**6. Composite**
Modern browsers separate the page into layers (like Photoshop layers). The compositor thread assembles those layers into the final frame and sends it to the GPU. Animations that only affect `transform` and `opacity` can be composited without triggering layout or paint — this is why those properties are preferred for smooth animation.

blocks.question: CSS is described as 'render-blocking.' What does that mean in practice, and why can't the browser just skip incomplete CSS and fill it in later?
blocks.answer: Render-blocking means no pixels are painted until the entire stylesheet is processed. The browser can't skip ahead because CSS rules can override each other — a rule at the bottom of a file can change styles set at the top. If the browser painted before reading the full stylesheet, it would produce a visible 'flash' as overriding rules applied. The browser trades latency (waiting for CSS) for correctness (painting only once with all styles resolved). This is why large CSS files and render-blocking stylesheets in the document head are a real performance concern.

blocks.content:
**JavaScript Execution: V8 and the Event Loop**

JavaScript runs inside an engine — in Chrome and Node.js, that engine is V8 (Google's open-source JavaScript runtime). V8 compiles JavaScript to machine code using a JIT (just-in-time) compiler (a technique that compiles code to native machine instructions on-the-fly during execution, rather than ahead of time — making frequently-run code nearly as fast as compiled C). The first execution is interpreted; hot code paths are identified and recompiled to optimized machine code.

JavaScript is single-threaded. There is one call stack. This is not a bug — it eliminates an entire class of race conditions. But it means that any synchronous operation that takes a long time (a large sort, a synchronous HTTP request) blocks everything else, including UI updates. This is why "don't block the main thread" is a core browser performance axiom.

The Event Loop is the mechanism that makes JavaScript feel concurrent without threads:
1. Execute whatever is on the call stack
2. When the stack is empty, check the microtask queue (Promise callbacks — high-priority async callbacks that run before the browser can do anything else) — drain it completely
3. Check the macrotask queue (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O callbacks — lower-priority scheduled work) — run one task
4. Repeat

This means Promise chains resolve before timeouts, and a deeply nested Promise chain can still block the UI if it runs long enough.

**Why This Matters for Make**

Every time a user clicks Preview, this entire pipeline runs on code that an AI just wrote. The AI does not know the user's screen resolution, does not know their browser's rendering quirks, and does not know that a deeply nested flexbox grid will trigger an expensive reflow on every keystroke. Make's preview environment is essentially a bet that the generated code will be performant enough to feel instant.

The practical implications:
- Generated code that synchronously fetches data in the component body will block rendering
- Generated CSS that changes layout properties inside animations will cause jank
- Generated JavaScript that runs a synchronous loop over a large array will freeze the UI
- The preview iframe (a sandboxed page embedded within the main page) must load, parse, and execute all of this while the user watches

The quality bar for "working preview" is actually higher than it looks. The code doesn't just need to be syntactically valid — it needs to clear the entire browser pipeline without producing a broken or slow experience.

## How to Think About This

The browser pipeline is a sequence of dependencies, not a parallel system: each stage needs the previous one to finish before it can start. CSS blocks paint. Scripts block parsing. Layout must complete before pixels draw. This means a slow stage doesn't just slow itself down — it delays everything that follows. For Make, this is the key lens: when a generated preview feels slow or broken, ask which stage is the bottleneck, because the fix lives there, not in the stage where the symptom appears.

**Server-Rendered vs. Client-Rendered: Make's Fork in the Road**

In client-side rendering (CSR), the server sends a nearly empty HTML file and a JavaScript bundle. The browser downloads the JS, executes it, and the JS builds the DOM. First Contentful Paint is delayed until JS executes. React's standard model is CSR by default.

In server-side rendering (SSR), the server runs the JavaScript and sends completed HTML. The browser paints immediately. Then JavaScript loads and "hydrates" (attaches event listeners to already-rendered server HTML, making it interactive without rebuilding the DOM from scratch) the page. First paint is faster; the complexity of hydration is real.

Make must choose: does a generated app render on the client, the server, or both? This choice affects:
- Where the runtime lives (browser sandbox vs. server container)
- What the deployment target looks like
- How quickly previews appear
- Whether the output is a static file or requires a persistent server process

Most early-stage code-gen tools default to CSR because it's simpler — no server required, output is just static files. But CSR apps have worse SEO and slower initial load. The moment Make wants to support server components, database queries at render time, or meaningful SEO — it needs SSR infrastructure. That infrastructure cost is substantial.

---

blocks.question: A user reports that their Make preview feels "janky" — elements jump around after the initial load. A bug report says "layout shift." Which phase of the browser pipeline is most likely responsible, and what category of generated code would cause it?
blocks.answer:
Layout (reflow). Common causes in generated code: images without explicit width/height attributes (browser doesn't know their dimensions until they load, causing reflow when they do), dynamically injected content above existing content, or web fonts that load and cause text reflow. In generated code specifically, the AI often omits image dimensions and skeleton placeholders.

---

blocks.title:
Open Chrome DevTools → Performance tab. Record a 5-second session of a Make preview loading. Identify the four colored sections in the flame chart: blue (HTML parsing/scripting), purple (rendering/layout), green (painting), gray (compositing). Find the longest task. If it exceeds 50ms, it's a "long task" that blocked the main thread.

---
blocks.solution: You should see four colored bands across the timeline — blue for parsing and script execution, purple for layout/render, green for paint, and gray for compositing. On a typical React app preview, the blue scripting band will dominate the first second: this is the JavaScript bundle downloading and executing. If you see a single yellow or blue bar wider than 50ms, that's a long task — the browser's label for work that blocks the main thread long enough to delay a frame. The most common long task in AI-generated apps is the initial React component tree render, especially when it happens synchronously in the root component. If your longest task is under 50ms, the generated code is healthy from a threading perspective. If it's 100ms or more, look at what JS is running during that task — it's likely a large synchronous operation (a data transformation loop, a nested component tree rendering all at once, or an expensive calculation in the component body). A well-optimized preview should have no long tasks at all during idle scrolling — only during initial load.

blocks.prompt:
Explain the browser rendering pipeline to a non-technical Figma designer on your team who asked why previews sometimes "flash" before the correct layout appears. Your explanation should take less than 90 seconds.

---

questions: Think of a time a product you shipped had a performance problem that traced to generated code, template output, or third-party content. Which phase of the browser pipeline caused it — layout, paint, scripting — and why wasn't it caught before release?

When you've worked on preview or prototyping tools — even low-fidelity ones — where did the gap between 'looks right in the tool' and 'behaves correctly in the browser' tend to show up? What does that tell you about where Make's real quality risk lives?

---

blocks.content:
Every Make preview is the full browser execution pipeline running on untested AI-generated code — performance problems in generated output are not bugs in the preview system, they are failures of code quality that the platform must either prevent or gracefully surface.

---

blocks.prompt:
> If Make defaults to generating React — and React has specific behavior in this pipeline — what does that lock-in mean for the runtime environment that has to execute it? That's what F.2 covers.

---

reviewQuestions.question: The browser rendering pipeline has six stages. Which stage calculates the exact position and size of every element on the page?
options: Paint — this is where the browser determines what to draw in each layer
Layout (Reflow) — this is where box model calculations resolve and element positions are determined
CSSOM Construction — this is where CSS rules are matched to DOM nodes and computed styles are assigned
reviewQuestions.explanation: Layout (also called reflow) is the stage where the browser traverses the Render Tree and calculates the exact position and size of every element. Margin, padding, width, height, and grid/flexbox algorithms all resolve here. Layout is expensive to trigger repeatedly — it must complete before the Paint stage can begin filling in pixels.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's right — Layout is the stage where positions are calculated, and it's one of the most expensive stages to trigger unnecessarily.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Paint fills in pixels; CSSOM assigns computed styles. Layout (Reflow) is the stage where the browser calculates exact element positions and sizes — every margin, padding, and grid algorithm resolves here. This stage must complete before the browser knows where to put any pixels.

reviewQuestions.question: Why is CSS described as 'render-blocking' in the browser pipeline?
options: CSS rules can override each other, so the browser must process the entire stylesheet before painting anything — painting partial styles would cause a visible flash as later rules applied
CSS blocks JavaScript execution, and since JavaScript builds the DOM, rendering cannot begin until CSS is processed
Large CSS files take a long time to download, and the browser pauses rendering until the download completes
reviewQuestions.explanation: CSS is render-blocking because a rule later in a stylesheet can override a rule earlier in it. If the browser painted before reading the full stylesheet, it would produce visible flashes as overriding rules applied. To avoid this, the browser waits until the entire CSSOM is built before producing any pixels. This is a correctness tradeoff — the browser delays rendering to paint only once with all styles resolved.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — render-blocking is about correctness, not download time. The browser is protecting against visible flashes from overriding rules.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The blocking is about rule override behavior, not download time or JavaScript interaction. Because a CSS rule at the bottom of a file can change styles set at the top, the browser can't safely paint anything until it has processed the entire stylesheet. Painting with incomplete CSS would cause visible flashes — so the browser waits.

reviewQuestions.question: In JavaScript's event loop, which of the following correctly describes the order in which callbacks are processed?
options: Microtasks (Promise callbacks) drain completely before any macrotask runs — the event loop processes the entire microtask queue after each call stack emptying
Microtasks and macrotasks are processed in parallel since the V8 engine uses multiple threads for async callbacks
Macrotasks (setTimeout, setInterval) run first in FIFO order, then microtasks (Promise callbacks) run in between macrotasks
reviewQuestions.explanation: The event loop always drains the microtask queue completely before picking up any macrotask. After the call stack empties, every pending Promise callback runs before the next setTimeout or setInterval fires. JavaScript is single-threaded — there is no parallel processing of queues. This ordering means that deeply chained Promises can starve the UI update cycle, causing jank even without explicit synchronous blocking.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — the microtask-first ordering is a subtle but important property that explains why Promise-heavy code can still produce an unresponsive UI.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: JavaScript is single-threaded — no parallel queue processing. The event loop always drains microtasks (Promises) completely before running any macrotask (setTimeout). This matters for Make because a long chain of Promises resolving back-to-back can block the UI update cycle even though the code looks asynchronous.
